items.passage: Ribosomes are microscopic factories inside every living cell that assemble proteins from coded instructions. They read the long message that is carried by a strand of messenger RNA. As the chain grows, each set of three letters adds one new amino acid in turn. Joined together in order, these small parts fold into the proteins that do vital work. Without this steady supply, a cell could not grow, repair itself, or stay alive for long.

Kudzu is a fast-growing vine from Asia that has overrun large areas of the southern United States. Originally introduced to control soil erosion, the plant soon escaped from the fields where it was planted. Under warm conditions it can extend nearly a foot each day during the active summer season. Smothering trees and fences alike, the dense leaves block the sunlight that native species need. Removing this aggressive invader now costs landowners enormous amounts of money and effort every year.

Selective attention is the mental process that lets people focus on one signal while ignoring others. At a crowded party, listeners can follow a single voice despite the noise that surrounds them. The brain filters out irrelevant sounds and sights so that limited resources are not wasted. Yet a familiar word, such as one's own name, can instantly capture attention from across the room. Psychologists study this ability to understand how the mind manages the flood of daily information.

The agricultural revolution transformed scattered bands of hunters into settled communities that grew their own food. Instead of wandering in search of game, people began to plant seeds and tend small fields. This shift allowed families to store surplus grain and support far larger populations than before. With a steady food supply secured, some members were freed to develop crafts, trade, and government. Historians regard this gradual change as one of the most important turning points in human history.

Ice ages are long periods when thick sheets of ice spread across vast regions of the planet. They happen because slow shifts in the Earth's orbit change how much sunlight warms the surface. As the air cools, snow piles up year after year and packs down into solid sliding ice. Moving over the land, these glaciers carve deep valleys and drop rocks far from their source. Geologists read such ancient marks to rebuild the climate of the distant frozen past.

The Moon's gray surface preserves a detailed record of violent collisions that happened billions of years ago. Countless craters formed when asteroids and comets struck the ground at tremendous speed long ago. Because the Moon lacks both wind and water, these scars remain almost unchanged across the ages. Dark smooth plains, called maria, mark places where ancient lava once flooded the lowest basins. By studying these features, scientists piece together the early history of the entire solar system.
